quote: No. I've been interested for a long time. Prices are coming down, and some of the offsets being offered by the state are attractive.
stewart_read: Frederick volunteers a substantive interest signal — long-standing curiosity, price trajectory awareness, state incentive knowledge. This is a qualified, informed customer, not a passive form-fill. The rep's response compresses it into a checkbox ('So you're just kinda looking into it now for the programs and just the extra benefits?'). A flip opportunity was also available here: Frederick's framing around prices coming down could have been redirected toward the cost of waiting — the longer he stays on his current bill, the more he's already paid. The rep took neither the depth nor the flip.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been intentionally keeping the verify section moving to avoid scope creep. But the codex is explicit that depth at interest_in_solar matters more than the answer — 60+ seconds is the target. At roughly 10 seconds, this is a miss, not a strategic compression.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex note on verify.interest_in_solar: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Then ask: what was Frederick actually telling you about his readiness? The manager should also introduce the objection_inversion flip for 'prices are coming down' — the cost of the current bill while waiting is the leverage point.

quote: We go between 3 and 6 quite regularly.
stewart_read: Frederick hands the rep a $300–$600 bill range with a $620 peak this month. The codex pattern is capture + flip — use the bill to create emotional alignment against the utility. The rep responds 'Yeah. Dang. Yeah. That is quite the range there. That's pretty crazy.' and moves on. The bill is captured and dropped. No flip, no emotional anchor, no 'that's exactly the kind of thing solar is designed to fix' bridge.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may be saving the emotional anchor for the closer's proposal conversation. Rejected — the setter's job is to create curiosity and urgency before the appointment, not to defer all emotional work to the closer. A bill this variable is a natural flip target.
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor coaching gap the codex names explicitly: 'Across all 7 deep-read calls, every rep captured the bill anchor and NONE flipped it.' Use this moment in the 1-on-1 to practice the flip: suggested rephrase intent — 'That range is exactly the problem — you're paying for the utility's variability, not your actual usage. Solar locks that in.' No fabricated numbers needed; the pain is in the range itself.

quote: can I get my magnifying glass here? Read the properly. Probably around 3,000 k w a year.
stewart_read: Frederick spends roughly 90 seconds reading his bill aloud, self-corrects, fetches a magnifying glass, and works through the kWh figures with genuine effort. This is the call's highest engagement moment and it is entirely customer-initiated. The rep accepts the data and immediately pivots to math ('3,000 times 12'). The effort Frederick put in is an implicit commitment signal — he is invested enough to do real work. The rep never acknowledges it.
coaching_implication: The manager should point to this moment as a missed micro-trade opportunity. Frederick did the rep's job for him. A brief acknowledgment — suggested intent: 'I appreciate you digging that out, that's exactly what we need' — would have reinforced the commitment and warmed the close. The rep moved past it like a data entry task.

quote: all we would need to do now is, yeah, just have someone stop by, share that design with you
stewart_read: The rep uses in-person language ('stop by') for what is a phone appointment. This is not a minor phrasing slip — it creates a concrete expectation mismatch. Frederick may be expecting a home visit on Wednesday at 2:30. If the closer calls by phone, Frederick's first reaction may be confusion or distrust, not engagement.
coaching_implication: The manager should ask the rep directly: did you mean to say 'stop by'? If it was a slip, the fix is simple — the button_up template specifies 'phone appointment' explicitly. If the rep is unclear on the appointment format, that's a training gap. Either way, the closer needs to be aware of this going into Wednesday's call.

quote: Can you I'm I wanna put my wife on the cell on the speaker. Okay. She's our available tomorrow. Yeah. She's our master scheduler.
stewart_read: Frederick voluntarily introduces his wife as the scheduling authority and puts her on speaker. The wife participates in ruling out Tuesday, and the Wednesday slot is selected with her input. This is a positive signal — she is engaged. But the rep never explicitly confirms she will be ON the Wednesday appointment. The codex is unambiguous: co-decision-makers must be confirmed present, not just consulted on timing. The rapport move ('My wife is the same way') is warm but does not substitute for the protocol.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have assumed that because the wife participated in scheduling, her presence on the appointment was implied. Rejected — implied presence is not confirmed presence. The codex distinguishes between influencing scheduling and being confirmed as an appointment participant.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk the rep through the protocols.spouse_decision template: 'Let's make sure [name] is on the appointment with us.' The rep had the wife on speaker — this was the easiest possible moment to get that confirmation. The closer walks into Wednesday not knowing whether the wife will be there.

stewart_read: The appointment is booked at Wednesday 2:30. But four of the six required button_up elements are absent: one_hour_block_stated, no_driving_at_computer, is_that_fair_handshake, and personalized tieback. The two present elements are appointment_datetime_confirmed (08:09) and text_followup_promise (08:17–08:41). The missing elements are not cosmetic — the no-driving/computer anchor and the is-that-fair handshake are the codex's primary no-show rate predictors.
coaching_implication: Run the button_up checklist with the rep against this call. The manager should name each missing element specifically: one_hour_block_stated, no_driving_at_computer, is_that_fair_handshake, personalized tieback. Ask the rep: which of these do you think matters most for whether Frederick shows up Wednesday? The is-that-fair handshake is the verbal contract — skipping it leaves the appointment as a calendar entry, not a commitment.

quote: just with that day and time, you know, just so it doesn't creep up on you guys.
stewart_read: The rep promises a follow-up text but frames it generically ('just with that day and time'). The codex requires a personalized tieback — something specific Frederick said on the call. Frederick mentioned his $620 bill, his dual 200-amp service, his long-standing interest in state incentives. Any of these would have made the text a reinforcement of the conversation rather than a calendar reminder. A generic text is a missed retention tool.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the text_after_call template and the required_elements note: 'tieback to interests or concerns.' Ask: what would you put in that text for Frederick specifically? The manager should have the rep draft the text in the 1-on-1 using something Frederick actually said — the $620 bill or the state incentives are the obvious anchors.

quote: Yes. Our son our daughter and son.
stewart_read: Frederick discloses that his daughter and son are on the title at the verify stage. The rep acknowledges and moves on without flagging that co-owners on title need to be present at the appointment. This is the first touch of the wife/co-owner pattern — the rep had the information at 01:20 and did not act on it. By 07:33, the wife surfaces as the scheduling authority, but the adult children on title are never addressed at all.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been treating the adult children as passive title-holders rather than active decision-makers. Possible — but the codex's protocols.adult_child_decision_maker is explicit: if an adult child is on the title, they should be included in the appointment. The rep had no basis to assume they were passive.
coaching_implication: The manager should connect this moment to the 07:33 wife moment as a multi-touch pattern: the rep had two opportunities to lock in co-decision-makers and took neither. The adult children on title are a separate exposure from the wife — the closer may arrive on Wednesday to find that the daughter or son is the actual financial decision-maker.
